message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this candidate finding. Independently check its evidence — READ the cited file/spec, or WEB-SEARCH the cited fact; do NOT trust the finding's own evidence text. Default to verdict "false" if you cannot substantiate it. If it is real but the stated fix is wrong or imprecise, mark "partial" and give a corrected refined_fix.

FINDING (JSON):
{"title":"Union key on one citizen + tier-blind claim understates federate compromise blast radius vs the memo's own bounding","location":"memo §Blast radius (lines 186-200)","type":"posture","severity":"low","issue":"The blast-radius section bounds the union-key risk as 'resting-key form is citizen-tier-only' and reassures that 'most citizens wear one hat.' But the multi-role union is the explicit feature (one citizen = one SA = union of capabilities, line 188-191), and the section frames the federate case as merely 'a hijacked live session rather than a file at rest' (line 197) — quietly weaker framing for the tier the whole heat exists to enable. A federate granted governor+director+retriever, hijacked mid-session, wields owner-equivalent authority with central revocation as the only backstop; the memo presents this as comparable-but-bounded without noting that the federation session cap (memo-20260527: 15min–12h, default 1h) is the only thing shrinking that window, and that a persisted refresh token (memo-20260527 test-rig residual) reintroduces a resting secret that re-creates the citizen-tier resting-key risk it claims federation removes. The 'tier-blind union property' is true, but the security *cost asymmetry* (resting file vs session+session-cap+possible refresh-token) is not surfaced where the reader weighs accepting the union.","evidence":"memo lines 196-198 frame federate exposure as 'hijacked live session rather than a file at rest'; memo-20260527 lines 117-121 (session cap 15min–12h) and 137-152 (refresh-token confined to test rig) show the bounding mechanism and the one residual that breaks it. The citizen memo's blast-radius section does not cite the session cap or the refresh-token residual as part of the federate union risk.","suggested_fix":"In §Blast radius, name the federation session cap as the mechanism bounding the federate union window, and flag the test-rig persisted refresh token as the one path that reintroduces a resting union secret — so 'union is tier-blind but the resting-secret cost is citizen-only' is stated with its single exception, not as an unqualified reassurance.","confidence":"low"}

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)

100	   is not involved.
101	3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.
102	
103	**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**
104	
105	```
106	POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
107	  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
108	  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
109	  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
110	  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
111	  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
112	```
113	
114	Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
115	no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.
116	
117	**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
118	1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
119	and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
120	long session.
121	
122	---
123	
124	## IdP landscape
125	
126	Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
127	Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
128	practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
129	The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
130	Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
131	IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.
132	
133	---
134	
135	## Refresh-token policy
136	
137	A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
138	Confine it to exactly one place:
139	
140	- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
141	  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
142	- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
143	  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
144	  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
145	  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
146	  token.
147	- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
148	  outside the operator mode enum.
149	
150	A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
151	on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.
152	
153	---
154	
155	## Where configuration lives
156	
157	Three homes, by scope:
158	
159	- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the

content.text:
Now I have the actual text. Let me carefully verify the finding's claims against what the memo actually says.

The finding claims the blast-radius section:
1. "understates federate compromise blast radius" 
2. "frames the federate case as merely 'a hijacked live session rather than a file at rest'" — quietly weaker framing
3. Does NOT cite the session cap or refresh-token residual

Let me check each claim against the actual text I just read.

The memo's blast-radius section (lines 193-200) says:
- "the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal); federation has no key at rest"
- "The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked live session rather than a file at rest"

Let me re-examine the finding's core argument. The finding argues that the memo frames the federate case as "comparable-but-bounded" but understates it. Let me read the finding closely.

Actually, the memo's framing is the OPPOSITE of what the finding alleges. The memo says the federate case is *less* exposed (no key at rest, only a hijacked live session), not more. The finding wants the memo to surface that:
1. The session cap is the mechanism bounding the federate window
2. The test-rig refresh token reintroduces a resting union secret

Let me check whether the memo's own text already addresses the resting-secret-at-federation point. The memo explicitly says "federation has no key at rest." But memo-20260527 lines 137-152 confirm a persisted refresh token IS a durable secret confined to the test rig. So the finding's factual claim that a refresh token reintroduces a resting secret in federation (test rig only) is substantiated by memo-20260527.

Let me check the "Verified facts" section and whether the memo already cross-references these elsewhere.

content.content:
1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.

214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241

content.text:
I've now read the full memo. Let me assess the finding carefully.

The finding's central claim is that the blast-radius section "understates federate compromise blast radius" and presents the federate case as "comparable-but-bounded" without surfacing the session-cap mechanism and the refresh-token residual.

Let me examine what the memo actually says versus what the finding claims:

**Finding claim 1:** The memo "frames the federate case as merely 'a hijacked live session rather than a file at rest' — quietly weaker framing for the tier the whole heat exists to enable."

Reality check: The memo's point in the blast-radius section is comparing *resting-key* exposure (citizen) vs *live-session* exposure (federate). The memo's framing is that federation is *less* exposed at rest (no key to steal). The finding tries to invert this into "understates federate blast radius." But the memo's framing is accurate and intentional: the section is about the *union-key resting-target cost*, which is genuinely citizen-only. A hijacked live session IS a meaningfully different (and generally smaller) exposure than a stealable file at rest, because it requires active compromise of a live session rather than exfiltration of a durable artifact.

**Finding claim 2:** The memo doesn't surface that the session cap is "the only thing shrinking that window."

This is the substantive part. The session cap is indeed the bounding mechanism for the federate window (memo-20260527 lines 117-121). The citizen memo's blast-radius section does not mention it. However — the federate is explicitly out of scope for this heat ("Federate (future, not this heat)", line 19). The blast-radius section is about the *citizen tier* union key — the cost the operator is being asked to accept *now*. The federate mention is a one-clause aside establishing that the union property is tier-blind, not a security analysis of federation.

**Finding claim 3:** The test-rig refresh token "reintroduces a resting secret that re-creates the citizen-tier resting-key risk it claims federation removes."

Let me scrutinize this. memo-20260527 lines 137-152 confine the persisted refresh token to the test rig only — "the single acknowledged durable-secret residual." The memo-20260527 itself already heavily qualifies "federation has no key at rest" with this exact residual. The citizen memo says "federation has no key at rest." Is that a real overstatement?

The citizen memo's claim "federation has no key at rest" is about the *shipped* path. memo-20260527 line 140: "Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token." The test-rig residual is firewalled to the project's own test org. So the citizen memo's claim is true for the shipped path, and the test-rig exception is a known, separately-documented residual that does not bear on the *operator-facing* blast-radius decision.

Now, the key question for severity and verdict: Is this a *real, substantiated, valuable* finding, or is it padding/over-reach?

The blast-radius section is about whether to accept the union key *for the citizen tier now*. The federate is explicitly future/out-of-scope. The one-clause federate aside ("a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked live session rather than a file at rest") is establishing tier-blindness of the union *property*, not making a security guarantee about federation. Asking the citizen-tier blast-radius section to additionally analyze the federation session cap and the test-rig refresh-token residual is asking it to pull forward a security analysis of a future tier that is documented elsewhere (memo-20260527) and explicitly deferred.

Let me verify there isn't a stronger version of this — specifically whether "federation has no key at rest" is a load-bearing claim that the union analysis *relies on* and that is contradicted by memo-20260527.

content.content:
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen

content.text:
I now have a thorough understanding of the memo. Let me make my final assessment.

The memo at line 146-147 already addresses the federation-no-key point in the orphan-hazard section: "Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this hazard is citizen-tier-specific." This is consistent with the blast-radius section.

Now let me evaluate the finding's verdict:

**The finding's factual premises that ARE substantiated:**
- The session cap (15min–12h, default 1h) exists and bounds the federate session (memo-20260527 lines 117-121). ✓
- The test-rig persisted refresh token is a durable secret residual (memo-20260527 lines 137-152). ✓
- The citizen blast-radius section does not mention either. ✓

**The finding's framing that is NOT substantiated / is over-reach:**

1. The finding claims the memo "understates federate compromise blast radius" and offers "quietly weaker framing for the tier the whole heat exists to enable." This is a mischaracterization. The memo's blast-radius section is about the *citizen-tier union-key resting target* — the cost the operator accepts NOW. The federate is explicitly out of scope ("future, not this heat", line 19). The one-clause federate aside establishes that the union *property* is tier-blind, which is true. It is not a security analysis of federation and does not claim to be.

2. The finding wants the memo to pull a federation security analysis (session cap mechanism, refresh-token residual) into the citizen-tier blast-radius section. But these are documented in memo-20260527 and pertain to a deferred tier. The blast-radius section is correctly scoped to the decision being made: accept the union key for citizens. The federate comparison is a "less exposed at rest" aside, not an unqualified reassurance about federation's overall posture.

3. The finding's strongest sub-claim — that "federation has no key at rest" is contradicted by the test-rig refresh token — does not hold. The claim is true for the shipped path (memo-20260527 line 140: "Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token"). The test-rig residual is firewalled to the project's own test org and is separately, explicitly documented as the single acknowledged residual. The citizen memo making a clean "no key at rest" statement about the shipped federation path, in a one-clause aside, is not an overstatement requiring correction.

4. The finding even acknowledges its own conclusion: "The 'tier-blind union property' is true." It then argues a *cost asymmetry* should be surfaced "where the reader weighs accepting the union." But the reader is weighing accepting the union *for the citizen tier* — and the cost asymmetry that matters there (resting file = citizen-only) is exactly what the memo already states ("the resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only").

The finding is asking the memo to import out-of-scope future-tier security mechanics into a section whose job is to justify a present citizen-tier decision. The memo is correctly scoped. This is a "would be nice to mention" at best, dressed up as an understated blast radius. It is not a real substantiated defect, and the strongest factual hook ("no key at rest" being false) does not survive scrutiny because the shipped path genuinely has no key at rest and the test-rig residual is explicitly caged elsewhere.

This is a "false" verdict: the framing of an understated/misleading blast radius is not substantiated, and the suggested addition pulls deferred-tier detail into a correctly-scoped present-tier section.

input.reasoning:
I read the cited memo section (citizen memo lines 185-200) and memo-20260527 lines 100-152 directly rather than trusting the finding's evidence text. The finding's individual facts check out (the session cap 15min–12h default 1h exists at memo-20260527:117-121; the test-rig refresh token is a durable secret at 137-152; the citizen blast-radius section names neither). But the finding's CHARACTERIZATION is over-reach and its load-bearing hook fails.

1. The finding claims the memo "understates federate compromise blast radius" with "quietly weaker framing for the tier the whole heat exists to enable." This mischaracterizes the section's job. The blast-radius section is about the CITIZEN-tier union-key resting target — the cost the operator accepts now. The federate is explicitly out of scope ("Federate (future, not this heat)", line 19-20). The one-clause federate aside ("a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked live session rather than a file at rest") exists to establish that the union *property* is tier-blind — which the finding itself concedes is true — not to make a security guarantee about federation.

2. The finding's strongest hook — that "federation has no key at rest" is contradicted by the test-rig refresh token, "re-creating the citizen-tier resting-key risk" — does not survive. memo-20260527:140 states "Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token"; the refresh token is firewalled to "the project's own test org only" (143-146) and is separately, explicitly documented there as "the single acknowledged durable-secret residual." The citizen memo's clean "federation has no key at rest" is true of the shipped path, and the test-rig exception is caged where it belongs. The citizen memo is internally consistent on this (cf. line 146-147 in the orphan-hazard section: "Federation has no key to leak ... so this hazard is citizen-tier-specific").

3. The finding asks the citizen-tier blast-radius section to import a future-tier security analysis (session-cap mechanism + refresh-token residual). That detail lives in memo-20260527 and pertains to a deferred tier. The cost asymmetry that actually bears on the present decision — resting-file exposure is citizen-only — is exactly what the memo already states ("the resting-key form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal)"). Adding the federation session-cap/refresh-token mechanics would pull deferred scope into a correctly-scoped section, against the paddock/memo division of labor.

Net: the facts are real but the alleged defect (understated/misleading federate blast radius, "unqualified reassurance") is not substantiated against the actual text, and the one factual contradiction it leans on ("no key at rest") is false for the shipped path it actually concerns.
input.refined_fix: No change needed. The blast-radius section is correctly scoped to the citizen-tier union-key decision; its federate clause is a true tier-blindness aside, not a federation security claim. "Federation has no key at rest" is accurate for the shipped path (memo-20260527:140), and the test-rig refresh-token residual is already explicitly caged in memo-20260527:143-152, the document that owns federation mechanics. If anything is wanted at all, it would be at most a one-token cross-reference ("federate session/secret posture: memo-20260527") — but the memo already cites memo-20260527 repeatedly for exactly this, so even that is redundant. Importing the session cap and refresh-token residual into the citizen blast-radius section would violate the paddock-holds-shape / memo-holds-mechanism-for-THIS-tier division and pull deferred federation detail forward.

content.text:
I've completed my adversarial verification of the finding and returned my structured verdict.

Verdict: **false**. The finding's individual facts are accurate (the session cap exists; the test-rig refresh token is a durable secret; the citizen blast-radius section mentions neither), but its core characterization — that the memo "understates federate blast radius" with an "unqualified reassurance" — does not survive reading the actual text. The blast-radius section is correctly scoped to the citizen-tier union-key decision being made now; the federate clause is a true tier-blindness aside, not a federation security guarantee. The finding's strongest hook ("federation has no key at rest" is contradicted by the test-rig refresh token) fails because that claim is true for the shipped path, and the test-rig residual is explicitly caged in memo-20260527, which owns federation mechanics.
